input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A building or infrastructure asset in the Architecture, Engineering, Construction and Operations (AECO) domain, represented as a cyber-physical information construct integrating static models with real-time runtime data to enable simulation, analysis, and optimization across the complete building life cycle from initial design to refurbishments and operational adaptations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Provides real-time monitoring through insights, predictive analytics for forecasting, infrastructure optimization, and enhanced decision-making capabilities for AECO stakeholders to reduce operational costs and improve collaboration throughout the building lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, though specific synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, and temporal granularity details are not provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Multiple digital twin instances are supported through a modular architecture with standardized extension points for custom service integration. A centralized architecture is employed with the DT Framework acting as a central hub for bidirectional data exchange, coordinating roles such as Building Control Engineers (device modeling), Facility Managers (real-time monitoring and control), and DT Engineers (service extension).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The DT Framework serves as a central hub orchestrating bidirectional data exchange across roles including Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers. Modular architecture with standardized extension points enables component integration, while runtime configuration management supports adaptability for holistic building lifecycle management.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability across heterogeneous systems is addressed via nonfunctional requirements (NF_X), with BIM tools utilized for device modeling by Building Control Engineers; however, specific data exchange protocols, external system dependencies, or integration patterns with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system employs Building Information Modeling (BIM) as a geometric foundation enhanced with behavioral models for Building Control System (BCS) components, including sensors, actuators, and controllers. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles transform enriched BIM models into structured digital twin artifacts, establishing a unified semantic foundation. Real-time data exchange mechanisms synchronize physical and digital components, enabling continuous state synchronization for operational monitoring and control.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Eclipse Hono and Ditto middleware facilitate continuous real-time data synchronization between physical assets and digital twins through sensor telemetry and BIM-enriched data streams. Bidirectional data exchange is supported, but specific data types beyond sensor and BIM data, transmission frequencies, preprocessing steps, and quality assurance mechanisms are not explicitly described.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control signals are transmitted from the digital twin to Building Control Systems (BCS) via runtime interfaces, enabling Facility Managers to operate physical infrastructure. Bidirectional communication mechanisms exist for control feedback, though command formats, validation, conflict resolution, and execution confirmation details are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Eclipse Hono and Ditto middleware enable real-time data exchange with standardized metadata handling, ensuring interoperability across heterogeneous systems. Specific network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, and security protocols are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Model-driven deployment with automated processes minimizes manual intervention, leveraging modular architecture and standardized extension points for scalable deployment. Runtime configuration management enables dynamic adjustments without full redeployment, supporting scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure systems.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle encompasses initial device modeling within BIM environments by Building Control Engineers, followed by Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) transformation to extract structured representations and automatically generate deployable artifacts. Subsequent phases include real-time data synchronization with physical infrastructure, interactive visualization for monitoring and control by Facility Managers, and extensibility through custom services developed by DT Engineers. Transitions between lifecycle stages are managed via automated MDE processes with minimal manual intervention, supported by role-based governance structures.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process leverages Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to transform enriched BIM models into structured representations, automatically generating deployable Digital Twin artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Evolution is managed via a modular architecture with standardized extension points, enabling integration of custom services and analytics to adapt to operational changes. Research projects TwinLight and BOREALIS inform the framework's requirements, though specific quality assurance, testing protocols, or version control mechanisms are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Real-time data exchange enables continuous synchronization between physical and digital systems, with interactive visualization providing immersive interfaces for Facility Managers to monitor and control infrastructure. Custom analytics services can be integrated through the modular architecture's standardized extension points, though specific analytical methods, decision-making workflows, or confidence metrics for recommendations are not specified in the documentation.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Standardization is implemented through standardized extension points in the modular architecture to ensure interoperability for custom service integration. While BIM models are utilized as a foundational element, their limitations for seamless DT deployment are acknowledged. However, no specific industry standards (e.g., ISO, IEC), compliance frameworks, or certification requirements are detailed in the provided documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the definition of the Digital Twin system provided in the text, describing it as a cyber-physical construct representing a building asset. Supported by quotes: 'Digital Twins (DTs) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static models with runtime data creates a virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization' and 'complete building life cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations during operation'.

The extraction correctly lists the specific services and benefits provided by the Digital Twin as described in the source. Supported by quotes: 'providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities' and 'benefits such as reduced operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life cycle'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

Highly specific and detailed explanation of the system's orchestration. It accurately synthesizes the central hub role, the coordination between actors, and the architectural patterns (modular, runtime configuration). Supported by quotes: 'DT Framework acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange' and 'Runtime configuration management further enhances adaptability'.

The extraction correctly notes that interoperability is addressed via 'nonfunctional requirements (NF_X)' and identifies BIM tools usage. It accurately states that specific data exchange protocols or integration patterns with external enterprise systems are not detailed in the text, despite mentioning Eclipse Hono and Ditto. Quotes: 'nonfunctional requirements (NF_X) address critical aspects of interoperability across heterogeneous systems', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning:
The extracted description is highly specific and accurately reflects the system's architecture described in the text. It correctly identifies BIM as the geometric foundation, the enrichment with Building Control System (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers), the use of Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) for transformation, and the real-time data exchange via Eclipse Hono and Ditto.

Relevant quotes:
- "model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment, grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)"
- "defining sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment"
- "leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts"
- "The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange"

The text mentions 'actuators' in the context of modeling requirements and references 'lighting systems' in related projects (TwinLight/BOREALIS). However, it does not provide the specific technical details required by the characteristic description, such as actuator types, control capabilities, ranges, safety constraints, or addressing mechanisms. Therefore, stating 'Not in Document' is correct based on the provided snippets.

Relevant quotes:
- "defining sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment" (indicates modeling, not physical specs)

The source text references 'sensors' and 'sensor configurations' and mentions the use of 'post-occupancy sensor data' in related projects. However, it lacks the specific details requested by the characteristic description, such as sensor types, measurement capabilities, spatial distribution, sampling frequencies, or accuracy specifications. Thus, 'Not in Document' is an accurate assessment.

Relevant quotes:
- "runtime semantics, such as sensor configurations... can be systematically embedded into BIM models"

The text briefly mentions a validation step and the use of the 'Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' for evaluation. It also references 'semantic drift' in related work. However, it does not describe specific model fidelity requirements, detailed validation and verification methods, uncertainty quantification, or error detection mechanisms. The mention of these topics is too superficial to fulfill the characteristic description, making 'Not in Document' appropriate.

Relevant quotes:
- "after validating the model [65]"
- "evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric"

The description correctly captures the MDE-based engineering process, the automated generation of artifacts, and the modular architecture for evolution. It accurately notes that the requirements are informed by TwinLight and BOREALIS, and correctly states that specific QA/testing details are absent. Quotes: 'Our approach automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms', 'rooted in two ongoing research projects, viz. TwinLight ... and BOREALIS'.

The description correctly identifies real-time data exchange and interactive visualization for Facility Managers as the primary decision support methods. It accurately states that custom analytics can be integrated via extension points, but specific algorithms or workflows are not detailed in the text. Quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'.

The description correctly identifies the use of standardized extension points for interoperability and the foundational role of BIM. It accurately notes the limitations of BIM for DT deployment and correctly observes that no specific industry standards (ISO, IEC) are detailed in the source text. Quotes: 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms', 'BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling paradigm', 'BIM however exhibits significant limitations when considering its application as a foundation for DTs'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source documents focus on model-driven engineering, BIM integration, and real-time data exchange workflows. While the text briefly mentions that MDE 'strengthens governance' regarding organizational policies, it does not contain specific details on data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, consent management, data anonymization, or data sharing agreements. Therefore, the extraction is correct in stating this is not in the document.

The source text describes the technical architecture using Eclipse Hono, Ditto, and Unreal Engine for connectivity and visualization. It lists non-functional requirements such as interoperability, scalability, and maintainability, but it does not discuss cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, or fail-safe mechanisms. Thus, the claim that this is not in the document is accurate.
